content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 26, 2018

Darlene K. Gee
Gene Orr
Orinda City Council
22 Orinda Way
Orinda, CA 94563

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-17-249

Dear Councilmembers Gee and Orr:

       This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the Act).¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict
of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as
common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. Moreover, this letter is based
on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when
it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Any advice we provide assumes your
facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions
should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTIONS

1. Is an Orinda Country Club membership an interest under the Act?

2. May Councilmembers Gee and Orr (collectively you) participate in City decision-
   making regarding a proposed downtown parking program that would affect
   properties within 500 feet of a portion of the 16th hole of the Club golf course?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes. The Orinda Country Club membership is a property interest and you also have interests
   in Orinda Country Club as an investment in a business entity.

2. Yes. You may take part in decisions regarding the downtown parking program because the
   decisions will not have a reasonably foreseeable material effect on your interests.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-249
Page No. 2

FACTS

A.     The City of Orinda.

Orinda is a 12.8 square mile, semi-rural community with a population of approximately
18,400. Downtown Orinda, the geographic center of the city, is split by Highway 24 and BART,
with the "Crossroads" area to the south and the "Village" area to the north.

B.     Orinda Country Club.

The Orinda Country Club, which was founded in 1924, provides its members and their
guests with access to a golf course, various recreational facilities, and other amenities on a 250-acre
property. According to maps provided, the 16th fairway of the golf course is adjacent to downtown,
but the Club's entrance, clubhouse and other primary structures are over 2,200 feet from downtown.
The Club is private and not open to the public. Except for the entrance area, the Club property is
fenced, including the area near downtown by the 16th fairway.

There are 508 parcels within a 500-foot radius of the Club's holdings. The vast majority of
these parcels are residential, but a water treatment plant is also included, as well as a few
commercial properties in the Village area. Unlike some other golf courses, the properties that
surround the Orinda Country Club are generally fenced off and separated from Club property.

A fence separates a portion of the Club's golf course from Orinda's downtown so that
people using the golf course cannot directly access downtown and vice versa. The Club's properties
in the vicinity of the Village area are not zoned for commercial development, and the City does not
consider it within the downtown area.

C.     Orinda Country Club Memberships.

The Club is a nonprofit mutual benefit association with multiple membership categories.
Members in each category have different levels of access to Club facilities and services, pay
different fees and dues, and have different privileges. The Club currently has a maximum of 560
"regular group" members and 275 "social group" members.

You each belong to the Club as regular group members, and neither of you serve on the
Club's Board of Directors. After application to and acceptance for Club membership, regular
members pay an initiation fee, and social members pay a social membership fee. Club bylaws do
not specify the amounts of these fees, which are periodically raised and lowered by the Club
Board based on supply of and demand for memberships, as well as other factors. Members also
pay monthly dues set by the Board. The value of membership, or financial interest in real
property, exceeds the $2,000.

You understanding that the Board's decision is not tied directly to the value of Club real
estate, but there may be an indirect link because interest in Club membership is potentially related,
at least in part, to the condition and value of Club real estate. Public tax information shows the
Club's net assets were approximately $15 million in 2015.

File No. A-17-249
Page No. 3

D.     Resignations and Dissolution.

When a member resigns from the Club, he or she must notify the Club's Board, which it
may reject until it approves a successor to take over the membership. Regular members have a
"proprietary interest" in their membership. Thus, if a regular member resigns and the membership is
transferred to an incoming regular member, the departing member receives a sum equivalent to the
new member's initiation fee, minus a "transfer fee." The Board sets the transfer fee amount. This
fee is currently two thirds of a regular member's initiation fee. The amount a regular member can
expect to receive upon resignation varies over time depending on the then-current initiation and
transfer fees set by the Board. Resigning members generally expect to receive less than they paid to
join the Club.

If the Club dissolves, the remaining value of Club assets and real estate, after payment of
debts and liabilities, would be divided among the current regular and social members. Regular
members would receive one share per capita and social members would receive one quarter share
per capita. Club bylaws identify this as its members' "property interest" in the Club.

E.     Downtown Parking Program.

The downtown parking program will soon be submitted to the City Council for
consideration and adoption. The Program includes a parking impact fee and other parking
management program elements for downtown Orinda. Specifically, the City would charge a new in-
lieu fee if a downtown development project does not provide sufficient on-site parking to meet the
City's parking standards. The City would use this revenue to mitigate the parking impacts of
downtown development, such as through acquisition of parking lots or spaces, and construction on or
participation in the construction of a small parking structure. The funds would not be used for any
other purpose other than parking projects.

You state that the in-lieu fee is not expected to incentivize development in the downtown
that would not otherwise occur, but rather to mitigate potential impacts associated with
development. The fee is proposed to apply only to potential development on commercial properties
in the downtown, not to residential properties or the Club.

There are currently no specific proposed parking improvement locations. However, because
of its location, the Club would not use current or future downtown parking. The Program would,
however, involve commercial properties in the City's downtown, a few of which are within 500 feet
of land owned by the Club. Club users do not park in the downtown to access the golf course or any
other Club amenities.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.² A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning of

_______________________________
² When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 (including a city council member) has
a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of

File No. A-17-249
Page No. 4

of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on
one or more of the public official's interests as set forth in Section 87103, which includes:

■ A business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or
more (Section 87103(a)), or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

■ Real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more.
(Section 87103(b), Regulation 18703.2.)

A. Real Property Interest.

We first address whether your interests in the Club constitutes a real property interest.
Section 82033 defines "interest in real property" to include "any leasehold, beneficial or ownership
interest or an option to acquire such an interest in real property located in the jurisdiction owned
directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public official, or other filer, or his or her immediate
family if the fair market value of the interest is two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. Interests in
real property of an individual includes a pro rata share of interests in real property of any business
entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially,
a 10-percent interest or greater."

In the Doering Advice Letter, No. A-12-068, we found that a proprietary membership in a
club, a nonprofit corporation registered as a 501(c)(7) non-profit mutual benefit organization, was
real property. We said that the proprietary members had an equitable right of ownership to the
club's real and personal property because they were entitled to a proportionate share of the value of
the assets if the club were to be dissolved. Doering also stated: "it is clear that, particularly when
the resale value of a club membership is determined at least in part by the value of the club's real
estate, the members have at least a beneficial interest in that real estate."

In this case, you would be entitled to a proportionate share of the value of the assets if the
Club were dissolved. Consequently, like the proprietary club membership in Doering, your
respective memberships constitute interests in real property.

B. Investment in a Business Entity.

The Commission has determined that club memberships can be an investment in a business
entity. Where the membership is in a "business entity" as defined in the Act, the membership has a
$2,000 value, and the membership may be resold for a profit or loss, we have advised that the
membership should be treated as an investment in a business entity. (Section 82034.)

In the present case, your memberships have a $2,000 value, they are transferrable, and they
can be resold for profit or loss. Consequently, you have an interest in the Club as a business entity
within the meaning of Section 87103(a).

the item, orally identify each type of interest involved in the decision as well as details of the interest as discussed in
Regulation 18702.5(b), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the
duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item.

File No. A-17-249
Page No. 5

C.     Foreseeability and Materiality – Downtown Parking Program.

     As discussed above, you each have a financial interest in your respective Club memberships under the Act. We must determine, therefore, whether the financial effect of those decisions on each of the respective interests is both foreseeable and material.

     Under the Act, an effect on an interest is presumed foreseeable if the interest is explicitly involved in the decision, and an interest is explicitly involved in the decision if the interest is a named party in, or subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) Your Club memberships are not "explicitly involved" in the decisions relating to the Program. Therefore, the effect of the decisions on their respective residences is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).)

1. Real Property Interests.

     Regulation 18702.2(a) sets forth the standards for determining whether the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's financial interest in real property is material. For real property not explicitly involved in a decision, a foreseeable effect is material if the decision:

     (10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest;

     (11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property, other than commercial property containing a business entity where the materiality standards are analyzed under Regulation 18702.1. Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission may provide written advice allowing an official to participate under these circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property; or

     (12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's property.

(Regulation 18702.)

     The Downtown Parking Program could result in changes in the downtown, and a portion of downtown is immediately adjacent to land owned by the Club. However, because of the physical separation between the Club property and downtown, an effect on the Club including the underlying property is rather unlikely. While the 16th fairway of the golf course is adjacent to downtown, the

File No. A-17-249
Page No. 6

Club's entrance, clubhouse and other primary structures are all over 2,200 feet from downtown. Moreover, Club users do not park in the downtown to access the golf course or any other Club amenities. Based upon the facts provided, there are sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on your property interests in the Club memberships from decisions regarding the Program.

2. Business Interests.

Regulation 18702.1 sets forth the standards for determining whether the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's financial interest in a business entity is material. For a business entity not explicitly involved in a decision, a foreseeable effect is material "if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock, or the value of a privately-held business entity." (Regulation 18702.1(b).)

The facts provided fail to demonstrate that the financial effect of your decisions would affect the Club's value. The facts do not show that the downtown parking program would contribute to a change in the value of the private Club.

Accordingly, you are not prohibited from taking part in the decisions.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 327-5525.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

By: John M. Feser Jr.
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest.2 A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning 
2 When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 (including a city council member) has 
a conflict of interest in a d,ecision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of  
of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on 
one or more of the public official's interests as set forth in Section 87103, which includes: 
• A business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of$2,000 or 
more {Section 87103(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 
• Real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. 
(Section 87103(b ), Regulation 18703 .2.) 
A. 
Real Property Interest. 
We first address whether your interests in the Club constitutes a real property interest. 
Section 82033 defines "interest in real property" to include "any leasehold, beneficial or ownership 
interest or an option to acquire such an interest in real property located in the jurisdiction owned 
directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public official, or other filer, or his or her immediate 
family if the fair market value of the interest is two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. Interests in 
real property of an individual includes a pro rata share of interests in real property of any business 
entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, 
a 10-percent interest or greater." 
In the Doering Advice Letter, No. A-12-068, we found that a proprietary membership in a 
club, a nonprofit corporation registered as a 50l(c)(7) non-profit mutual benefit organization, was 
real property. We said that the proprietary members had an equitable right of ownership to the 
club's real and personal property because they were entitled to a proportionate share of the value of 
the assets if the club were to be dissolved. Doering also stated: "it is clear that, particularly when 
the resale value of a club membership is determined at least in part by the value of the club's real 
estate, the members have at least a beneficial interest in that real estate." 
In this case, you would be entitled to a proportionate share of the value of the assets if the 
Club were dissolved. Consequently, like the proprietary club membership in Doering, your 
respective memberships constitute interests in real property. 
B. 
Investment in a Business Entity. 
The Commission has determined that club memberships can be an investment in a business 
entity. Where the membership is in a "business entity" as defined in the Act, the membership has a 
$2,000 value, and the membership may be resold for a profit or loss, we have advised that the 
membership should be treated as an investment in a business entity. (Section 82034.} 
In the present case, your memberships have a $2,000 value, they are transferrable, and they 
can be resold for profit or loss. Consequently, you have an interest in the Club as a business entity 
within the meaning of Section 87103(a).

Regulation 18702.5(b ), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himselfor herself; and (3) leave the-room for the 
duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. 


C. 
Foreseeability and Materiality- Downtown Parking Program.  
As discussed above, you each have a financial interest in your respective Club memberships 
under the Act. We must determine, therefore, whether the fi:nancial effect of those decisions on each 
of the respective interests is both foreseeable and material. 
Under the Act, an effect on an interest is presumed foreseeable if the interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision, and an interest is explicitly involved in the decision if the interest is a 
named party in, or subject of, the decision. (Regulation 1870 I (a).) Your Club memberships are not 
"explicitly involved" in the decisions relating to the Program. Therefore, the effect of the decisions 
on their respective residences is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701 (b ). ) 
1. Real Property Interests. 
Regulation 18702.2(a) sets forth the standards for determining whether the reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's financial interest in 
real property is material. For real property not explicitly involved in a decision, a foreseeable effect 
is material if the decision: 
(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially 
altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property 
surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or 
air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market 
value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest; 
(11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located within 500 
feet of the property line of the official's real property, other than commercial 
property containing a business entity where the materiality standards are analyzed 
under Regulation 18702.1.Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission may 
provide written advice allowing an official to participate under these 
circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to 
indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the 
official's property; or 
(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration 
under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a 
nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of 
the official's property. 
(Regulation 18702.) 
The Downtown Parking Program could result in changes in the downtown, and a portion of 
downtown is immediately adjacent to land owned by the Club. However, because of the physical 
separation between the Club property and downtown, an effect on the Club including the underlying 
property is rather unlikely. While the 16th fairway of the golf course is adjacent to downtown, the  
Club's entrance, clubhouse and other primary structures are all over 2,200 feet from downtown. 
Moreover, Club users do not park in the downtown to access the golf course or any other Club 
amenities. Based upon the facts provided, there are sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no 
reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on your property interests in the Club memberships from 
decisions regarding the Program. 
2. Business Interests. 
Regulation 18702.1 sets forth the standards for determining whether the reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's financial interest in a 
business entity is material. For a business entity not explicitly involved in a decision, a foreseeable 
effect is material "if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the price of the 
business entity's publicly traded stock, or the value of a privately-held business entity." (Regulation 
18702.l(b).) 
The facts provided fail to demonstrate that the financial effect of your decisions would 
affect the Club's value. The facts do not show that the downtown parking program would contribute 
to a change in the value of the private Club. 
Accordingly, you are not prohibited from taking part in the decisions.
